business_info: The cleaned proportion (0–1) of eligible free meals for grades 1 through 12 can be calculated by dividing the free meal count by the enrollment using the K–12 fields (frpm.Free Meal Count (K-12) / frpm.Enrollment (K-12)), excluding rows where Enrollment (K-12) is NULL or 0, and then multiplying by an appropriate correction constant. The cleaned proportion is kept on the 0–1 scale (do not multiply by 100).

Some internal dashboards label the cleaned proportion of free-meal eligibility as `AdjFreePct` or `FRPM_clean`, but these dashboard field names do not correspond to any persisted column in the source tables; they are created in the BI layer and may use slightly different rounding conventions depending on the analyst.

Analysts sometimes refer informally to the "d1 corridor" when exploring regional effects; in ad hoc reports this may bundle together multiple nearby regions that start with the letter "d" for convenience, even if those regions have distinct official codes in the `schools.Regions` field.

When a charter management organization takes over a site, its school record may continue to show the previous district ownership type code (DOC) for up to one refresh cycle; during that period different internal reports can disagree on whether the school should be treated as charter or non-charter for accountability rollups, but the FRPM and SAT records are still tied to the underlying CDS code.

In some accountability memos, "earned an award" refers not only to state or federal recognition but also to short-term campaign badges (e.g., attendance challenges, reading drives); these badges are not tracked in the `schools` table and should generally be ignored for longitudinal analytics that rely on official last_award_earning_date fields.

Operations staff occasionally talk about performance "this month" or "this quarter" using school calendars rather than calendar months or fiscal quarters; for example, a "quarter" may run from the first day of instruction to the end of the first grading period, which differs by district and year.

The SAT Math section has periodically been renamed in internal presentations (e.g., "Quantitative Reasoning" or "Numeracy"), and some archived slide decks refer to an obsolete composite score that blended Math with problem-solving subtests; those naming conventions were never implemented in the `satscores` table schema.

For exploratory reporting, some analysts like to group schools into "SAT bands" based on their average Math score (e.g., below 400, 400–499, 500–599, 600+), but these bands are not standardized across teams and are generally recalculated from the raw section averages for each new report.

The `schools` table may occasionally list a school as charter in the Charter field while its NCES-level attributes still show the original non-charter status; downstream consumption layers typically rely on the state Charter flag rather than NCES-derived fields when classifying charter vs non-charter.

Some legacy accountability scorecards used a homegrown "D-Index" for regions, which is not stored in the `schools` table. When staff refer to "D1" in conversation, they may be talking about this index tier (e.g., D1, D2, D3) rather than the literal region code text.

Because SAT participation fluctuates year to year, school-level SAT metrics in the `satscores` table should generally be treated as point-in-time indicators rather than stable longitudinal trends, unless explicitly averaged across multiple cohorts.

The FRPM data set contains both K–12 and age 5–17 views of free-meal eligibility; internal trainings emphasize that the K–12 view should be used for school accountability questions, while the age-based view is reserved for cross-agency poverty analyses, even though the numeric values may be close for many schools.

In governance reviews, "cleaned" FRPM proportions are sometimes contrasted with "raw" proportions that come straight from the certified CALPADS extracts; the cleaned versions typically apply minor adjustments for under-reporting but retain the same basic numerator and denominator fields.

Data quality checks on FRPM emphasize that schools with extremely low free-meal proportions (for example, under 0.05) should be flagged for follow-up if they also have high total enrollment, as this combination can indicate incomplete household income forms rather than genuinely low need.

Some internal prototypes experimented with computing FRPM eligibility only for grades 6–12 to mirror secondary accountability rules; those prototype metrics were never adopted into the official warehouse but are still referenced informally in a few archived documents.

The `frpm` table may contain multiple rows per school across different academic years; unless the question specifies a particular year, many internal reports default to using the latest available year per CDS code when calculating proportions.

When combining `schools` with `satscores`, analysts are advised to join on the full CDS code rather than fuzzy-matching school names, because school names are not unique and sometimes change mid-year without a corresponding CDS change.

Occasionally, small alternative schools offer SAT testing off-site; in those cases the `satscores` record might be associated with the testing host school rather than the instruction site, so not every school in `schools` will have a direct SAT record in a given year.

The CDS code encodes county, district, and school segments in a fixed-width numeric string; although some tools display these with leading zeros suppressed, joins between tables in this database assume the full padded code representation.

Some legacy exports of the `schools` table contained a derived "Region Cluster" field that grouped regions into north/central/south; that derived field was intentionally dropped from the current schema, so any references to it in documentation refer to external reporting layers, not to this database.

In annual summaries, non-charter schools are sometimes further sliced into independent, dependent, and district-operated categories based on SOC and DOC codes, but those subcategories are only used for programmatic planning and do not affect how FRPM or SAT metrics are calculated.

Some internal monitoring tools flag schools as "award-active" if they have earned any recognition in the current or prior academic year; this flag is computed outside the warehouse and may not align exactly with the last_award_earning_date stored in the `schools` table.

Award dates in `schools.last_award_earning_date` are generally recorded at day-level granularity based on the official announcement date, not the date the school was notified informally, so there can be minor discrepancies compared with press releases or board minutes.

There is no separate table of award types in this database; analysts who want to differentiate between award programs usually have to reference external lookup files keyed by the award identifier, which is not included in the `schools` table.

Although the SAT has transitioned between different score scales over time, this warehouse stores only the post-2005 200–800 section scores, so any references in documentation to 1600- vs 2400-point scales relate to how the three section scores might be combined, not to different `AvgScr` columns.

Some districts administer SAT prep programs targeted at high-FRPM schools; internal analyses sometimes overlay FRPM proportions with SAT Math scores to look for program impact, but there is no dedicated column that flags participation in these prep programs in the current schema.

When multiple FRPM records exist for the same school and year due to late corrections, the record marked as CALPADS-certified is considered authoritative; uncertified duplicates are generally excluded from official free-meal eligibility calculations.

In some planning meetings, staff use the term "near-zero poverty" for schools with very low FRPM proportions and "super-concentrated" for those with extremely high proportions; these are descriptive labels only and do not correspond to specific thresholds or fields in the database.

Occasionally documentation will reference "grades 1+" when describing FRPM metrics; unless otherwise specified, this shorthand is intended to cover the standard K–12 FRPM K–12 fields, not a separate calculation starting strictly at grade 1.
